yeah, in the know,

Churis.
Dr.

Evers, I was able to close the biggest deal with Siemens Mexico.

now y'all are looking to double that.

tell me like with Contently, how did it help with content creation?

create create content.
So the way that Contently worked, as mentioned, so the first step was, what is your content strategy look like?
So before even being able to work in the platform, we had to flesh that out from down to the intended audience that you're looking for, the tone, all of that.
We were very critical in understanding exactly what you're looking to achieve.
From there, we would target SEO keywords.
So you would upload whatever SEO keywords you may have currently into our platform, and then we can also add as many as you would like to.
And then we had different things as far as just themes, like what does the tone look like?
What does that message kind of look like?
And once all of that is completed, it now takes an aggregate of your profile and provides you different freelance.
influencers that you can choose to, you know, work with on top of whoever you may have in-house.
And so within that platform, you're able to see their profile, who all they've worked with, what type of content they've done, et cetera.
You can choose as many as you would like, and they're kind of like, as mentioned, outside counsel in a sense, where you're just paying them for the time if you decide to work on a project.
So from there, after that, we also have a segment where we can provide suggestions for titles.
Let's just say, for instance, you don't know which way or type of direction you want to go with.
Based off of all of the keywords that you have in your intake, our freelancers will provide different suggestions.
So now that you can have a quick title to kind of have some type of format and template before you start building.
Once you select that, you can approve, you can disapprove.

From there, you go into the content workflow command center, and then it allows you to see if things are, what is the thing called when you were checking to see if they've copywritten it or it was AI driven and creating JGBT plagiarism.

We had broken links to make sure that the information that they tell you is verified.
Make sure that the SEO keyword count is there.
Does it meet your theme?
Does it meet your tone?
Replace the words with that, like the full thing.

On top of that, we had a campaign ability.
So if you had a specific campaign, you can track and measure the metrics on that and test it.
And then finally was the analytics.

So based off of the analytics, over time, you're able to see compared to purchasing, you know, advertising and different space through Google Play, you could track to see what does that organic growth look like, how much it would have cost based off of, you know, the traffic that you've generated.
What you need to improve on.
You can see which one of the freelancers actually, you know, drive audience engagement and things of that nature.

much.

SDR team off and we had to prospect as well on our own.

On, like, the...

Outbound topic, one thing that we want to learn more about is like, and you've touched on Project Longhorn, which is great, like, tell us about like an outreach experiment or campaign that you ran that like consistently generated meetings.

So it could be Project Longhorn, it could be something different, but just help me kind of understand like how the idea came about and then your process of going about and executing on standing up, right, that Outbound campaign.
Yeah, as mentioned, just since we're on the top of Project Longhorn, we had an opportunity to really focus on a vertical and I had success with a couple of financial institutions.
So I decided to choose, you know, that vertical from there.
I understood like within Texas, they like to do business with Texas citizens, I would like to say, in a sense, just that Texas pride.
I worked in a digital media marketing company, so I really had a lot of experience.
With credit unions and banks.

And so from there, what I did, I mapped out, you know, all of the financial institutions within Texas, was able to see 186 that met the threshold of what we're looking for from an annual revenue generated perspective, as well as TAMP.
Once I was able to do that, I then segmented it based off of industry challenge and what most of their objectives were.
Some of the things that I was able to pinpoint was the fact that, you know, they were looking to expose and expand their brand depth from a department standpoint.
So different businesses that they have and different business lines within the banks, because most people just think of, you know, you're checking savings.
But there's so many different outlets within a financial institution.
On top of that, they were looking to...
Also address the point that they were not, you know, the baby boomers were dying down and, you know, millennials.
So they wanted to expand and get new audience.
So you're Gen Z, you're Gen Alpha.
What can we do to kind of attract and get them because they're the ones who will have the money moving forward?

Once I was able to kind of understand that was the industry challenge and, you know, that digital transformation that they were looking to do, I created a campaign that was specific to CMOs, content, head of content, VP of marketing.
Those were the three main people that those messages and sequences were directed towards.
They're, you know, kind of campaign and map out, you know, six to eight different individuals within an institution.
So, you know, of course, most three would be there, but then the other three could be.

You know, a director or somebody with some type of cachet that may be a user, not an economic buyer, but a user for the product who could also champion and kind of speak to what their day-to-day looks like.
So the involvement as far as just required steps would be LinkedIn invitation followed by a personal message.
After the personal message, then I create, you know, those sequences for those companies.
After a sequence, I follow up with a phone call to each one.
After the phone call, if I'm unable to pick up, I leave a voicemail.
After the voicemail, then it's just a follow-up email and then an individual invitation to one of our webinars.
Once that doesn't work, it's followed by an individual message, again, via LinkedIn.
And so you kind of repeat that process from ABM standpoint and...
Track and measure.
And as you know, it takes, what, 16 to 18, or 18 to, I think, 24 touch points before you get the attention of somebody.
And once I was able to do so, I actually got somebody to join one of the webinars.
So I followed up, asked them what their thoughts were, because we kind of saw ourselves as thought leaders in the space.
And so from that, they also downloaded one of our handbooks, and then I was able to really engage.
That's where that conversation with Pinnacle came and led to, you know, that sell.
But, you know, that repetitive motion and, you know, just staying very strategic and meticulous in your approach is really the secret sauce to success.
It is boring, and sometimes you feel like, you know, you're doing this all for nothing, but the average law of action.
Averages and, you know, playing the numbers game, eventually someone will pick up.
So that's where that, you know, strategy came into play and I was able to be successful.
That's awesome.
Yeah, I totally agree.

And that's sometimes the hard part day to day when you're dialing.

yourselfท it students

know that.

Let's move into like, you're in the sales cycle, right?
And it could be at Contently or it could be at a different place.

But tell me about like how you position the product's value to a CMO in like a deal or like a go-to-market leader, if not a CMO, but like, how do you, how did you position the product's value to that buyer and like a deal that you closed?
Yeah.
So one of the ways that I'm able to position value is really just understanding what is the business outcome that you're looking to achieve.

So, you know, during the discovery calls, I'm really asking, you know, those tough questions to see, not from a feature standpoint, but how does this help solve you all's business initiative that you're looking to achieve?
So some of the things that you're looking to.
Gain, especially with like CMOs.
They're looking to see results from traffic, gain of traffic, trying to see conversions from their own pipeline as well.
So how many people are actually converting and getting these type of deals?
And then, of course, you know, traffic increase is another thing that they're looking to do.
So when I'm tailoring my messaging and also building that business case, I'm trying to ask them, like, what are you looking to achieve?
What does that look like from an ROI standpoint?
And kind of mapping it backwards to tell them how our solution can increase their chances of hitting that goal that they're looking for.
Nice, nice.
And I guess is Contently the best example to go to product-wise?
Because CMOs were the exact personas and titles that I was approaching within that tenure.
But let's just say doctors, the same thing would work for them.
I would be speaking to CLOs instead or general counsel or procurement.
Like with Siemens, what I was able to do, I had to sell to three different departments, IT, procurement, and legal.
So the thing that they were looking to solve for was to replace their manual contract workflow.
And the biggest thing with legal is they were stolen on deals.
They were looking for a way to close deals faster.
So when you're able to review with, you know, our solution, we're able to allow you all to get the language that you're looking for with a simple click of a button.
From a procurement standpoint, what they were looking to do was understand what they were liable for and what was agreed upon during the signed agreement.
Another thing that was a big talking point was holding themselves accountable.
So when deals did stall or the contract review process, who's holding up the process, they didn't have a way to automatically track that.
So that's where that repository segment came into play.
And then from an IT standpoint, because of AI, they wanted to ensure that their data wasn't being shared.
They wanted to ensure that the AI had a good turnaround time or didn't really drift from what they were looking for.
So those were the different things that I had to sell to and then build different business cases for each one.
So I want to say, like they were saying, if we can, you know, reduce our first pass review from one week to a couple of days, that would be a godsend for them.
From a procurement standpoint, they were saying when contracts are about to expire and.
Automatically renew.
They wanted to have a way to track that ahead of time because that could cost them X amount of million of dollars if they decide to keep a product that they had no intentions of renewing.
But because they don't have the right signals or the data in front of them, they had little gap slip ups and things of that nature.
So another big thing was tariffs with the new tariffs.
So we had this AI agent that could read all of the contracts, let's just say for NDAs.
There's a force majeure clause.
We can create like a tab to let them know, hey, based off of the signed agreement, you do have a force majeure.
And it allows you to opt out of this contract due to the price increase resulting in tariffs.
So things in that nature were some of the things that they were looking for to help them with their day-to-day.
Nice.
Yeah, that's crystal clear.

Yeah, mean, Siemens was one.
kind of went into detail with that, but another one that I would like to talk about would be Sekisui with Plangistics.
It wasn't even a SAS product.
We did more of a service, so we helped resin manufacturers reduce the degraded resin material that they may have had during production.
The way about this deal came about, I had to cold call.
I do remember doing ride-arounds with...
The vice president of sales, and he had told me that that was a deal that was very close to closing, but their POC, point of contact, moved to North Carolina or to New York.
She was pregnant, moved, and they lost contact and never had another opportunity.
So what I did was made a number of phone calls, and with plants and manufacturing facilities, you have to really do a good job at getting in front of the gatekeeper.
So utilizing Salesforce, I kind of just spat out a couple of names.
They transferred me to a couple of people, and I was able to leave a voicemail, and you don't even want to know how that voicemail came about.
It was so funny, but nonetheless, the task at hand was to connect with somebody.
was able to connect with them, and because of the sales cycle, typical sales cycle for this deal, lasted anywhere between 9 to 12 months.
I was really building a relationship with them.
Understanding what their challenges were.
For them, they had like six different levels of grade that were degraded all the way to where they're not even able to salvage it.
They just throw it away.
And they were looking for a way to improve that process so that they can at least get some sense on the dollars for, you know, this like C-level or D-level resin material.

Some of the steps included was bringing them to our facility, giving them a tour, letting them know of our other products and offerings, and then showing them the actual facility where we can do all the cleaning.
I had requested testing.
So one of the product offerings that we're able to do, very similar to a POC, we're able to ask them to give us the material.
We'll test it out for free and provide you the sample of what that looks like and show you exactly how well our product would.
Work.
Once we did all of that, I was able to consult with the logistics supply chain manager because, again, you know, some of the times, a lot of these are coming in different rail carts.
And I believe at the time to ship it from like Texas to like California, they were spending like 80 cents per rail cart.
And you got to understand a cart itself typically was 184,000 pounds.
So per pound times 80 something cents, it comes out to a pretty hefty load for them.
And so if we were able to guarantee to get their throughput a certain level, we can offer them a significant discount, not only from a storing perspective, but also shipping it out.
So if I'm not mistaken, we were able to reduce it from 80 cents to close to about like 35 cents, and then also provide them warehouse storage.
And eventually they decided to sign off on it.
And with our deals, it wasn't like, hey, you are, you know, this is what we're expected.
They gave us a number of what their throughput was and what all of them, we kind of take an aggregate of what that would look like from a year's date.
So that accumulated to close to about almost $400,000 from a standpoint because of the throughput level that they were looking at, was able to close that deal.
It took about like nine and a half months, but that was one of the longest deals.

But really just taking those necessary steps, understanding what they were looking to achieve, bringing in the right people, and, you know, going through that full sale cycle is something that I do remember.
You know, I could go about Siemens, but I kind of gave you detail, but those are too big, really.
Memorable deals that I had to close.

Um.

No, I've actually only been here like three or four months.
Yeah, it's been, it's felt like two years for sure.
But, you know, I had been the founding AE at a company called Air and they sold like a creative operations platform.
So I was selling to marketers and creatives.
And then at Cerros, you know, you're selling to marketers and creatives, but it's actually more about using the platform to create interactive content.
So I think it was really like the combination of those two things.

Because we use people to perform our service and we're building these products that we're launching in Q1.
And so I think, like, the hardest thing is, like, how do you be successful while you're also trying to build all these processes alongside closing deals?
And how do you, like, be successful when there's no answer to the question that you might have?
So you have to start sort of breaking down that question into, well, where would I go, right, to, like, find some of that information?
Or who would I reach out to versus try to, like, spend 30 minutes on this on my own?
And also just like being comfortable with the fact that, you know, I may have to jump into a sales conversation and not be all that prepared.
But if I know what we want to talk about, right, to some of the things you said, right, digging into what's the outcome they're trying to drive?
Well, you know, what's their current state?
What's their future state?
What's in the way?
What's like the required capabilities to get to that future state?
Then you can fit your solution in really nicely, you know, around that.
So I think in short, it's just like we have so much need to build a process here, but we also have to close two, three deals every week.
So it's like, how do I do both of those things at the same time when there's no playbook to go on out and do it?
From that standpoint, what does sales cycle look like and deal size typically?
Yeah, so we break our engagements into like this eight-week strategy sprint that typically costs 30 to 40K and it's a flat.
like, Thank
And then clients have until the last week of that eight-week sprint to opt out of the 12-month growth execution that starts at $15,000 per month.
So for the $15,000 per month, that's $180,000, and that's our average deal size.
But the key is you've got to find the client that's going to be the right fit and that understands our process well enough.
You've got to qualify them enough, not just try to close it, so they can get paid out on phase two, right?
Like phase two is much more of a moneymaker than phase one.
So yeah, $180,000 is like the average deal size.
And I mean, every chief marketing officer is thinking about how do I show up in AI search?
What's GEO?
What's AEO?
So in terms of sales process, you're selling the strategy sprint.
And you can get them to the strategy sprint after one, two, three at the most calls.
But the key is if you can slow down to speed up, if you can slow down to set up our delivery team to be more successful.
Well, then you can guarantee getting to that phase two, right?
Which is where the big deal comes in.
But you sell it all up front, which is nice.
They just, they can opt out until the last week.
I guess to kind of play on that question, what does that conversion typically look like from year and once they are in week seven?
Yeah, that's a good question.
We started off real hot and we were almost at like 100% conversion, but then we tried to scale.
And we were saying yes to these clients who were like incredibly technical, but like cool logos.
So base 10 is an example.

If you look at base 10, like they're growing really fast, but that's an example of one that like didn't convert to phase two, because even though our point of contract was really interested, like the CMO, the CEO, like they had different goals for how technical the subject matter should be.
how comfortable they were for like publishing at a scale on their blog, because we need like a lot of scale to drive results.
You know, like.
That's part of our process is we're going to go in and try to create an article every single business day.
And so I would say that it's too early to give you like definitives around conversion to phase two, but it's more clients for sure convert than don't convert.
We did have like a bad month, you know, like two months ago where three clients, right, out of like maybe 13 didn't convert.
But that's just because we should have done better qualification up front, right?
It's like something I'm learning like as I'm onboarding, right, which is not easy.
But I would say, you know, we've done a lot of cross-functional work and that's like partly why I'm here this week.
And the key is just like, you know, hey, if we can define what a great client looks like, there's like five clients that we could choose from, right?
So like we're not having challenges closing deals.
We just got to make sure that if we can only do two or three, we choose the right two or three from those five or six.
Understood.
Okay.
That is it for me, I guess.
I know that we're...
A little over time.
What does next steps look like?
would be curious to know.
Yeah, for sure.
So Tucker and Bridget are kind of the main folks involved in the process.
Bridget's our VP of Operations.

I'll give my feedback to Tucker, and then he'll connect with Bridget, and the next interview would be with Bridget, and Tucker would, I guess, communicate to you once they've decided where you go from here.
But it'd be an interview with Bridget, and then like a team interview where you do more of like a presentation.
Okay, got you.
Fair enough.
Well, I wish you the best out there.
You said San Francisco, right?
Yeah, I'm in Houston, but in San Francisco this week, yeah.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
So safe travels out there.
If we do connect, hopefully we can play ball and then, you know, talk strategies.
So looking forward to it.
That sounds great, man.
Great to meet you, Lewis, and appreciate the time.
Um, best of luck to you and, uh, we'll be in pitch soon.
Okay.
Bless.
See ya.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing Louis Ukwu for the Account Executive role.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=396.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Relevant Experience:** 10+ years as an enterprise AE, including a role at Contently selling a content marketing platform.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=473.0)
  - [**Outbound Success:** Developed "Project Longhorn," an ABM campaign that increased qualified leads by 38% and landed a \~$184k deal with Pinnacle.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1080.0)
  - [**Complex Deal Management:** Closed a multi-year, six-figure deal with Siemens Mexico by multi-threading across legal, procurement, and IT departments.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1630.0)
  - [**GrowthX Model:** Sells a two-phase engagement: a $30-40k strategy sprint followed by a $15k/mo execution retainer, with an average deal size of $180k.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2624.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Background & Motivation

  - [**Experience:** 10+ years as an enterprise AE, selling to C-level executives.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=473.0)
  - [**Recent Roles:**](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=423.0)
      - [**Dr. Churis:** AI contract review platform.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=423.0)
          - [**Outcome:** Laid off after the company failed to hit Series B funding goals.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=455.0)
      - [**Contently:** Content marketing platform.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=523.0)
          - [**Outcome:** Left after the company was sold and the team was let go.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=928.0)
  - [**Motivation for GrowthX:**](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=410.0)
      - [Direct experience selling a similar content platform at Contently.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=523.0)
      - [Desire to join a high-growth startup (0→3M→7M ARR) and contribute to its doubling.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=621.0)

### Contently Product & Process

  - [**Solution:** A platform and freelance network for end-to-end content creation.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=563.0)
  - [**Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=647.0)
    1.  [**Strategy Intake:** Define client goals, audience, and tone.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
    2.  [**SEO & Themes:** Upload keywords and define content themes.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=688.0)
    3.  [**Freelancer Network:** Platform matches clients with vetted freelancers.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=712.0)
    4.  [**Workflow & QA:** Manage creation with plagiarism/link checks and SEO optimization.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=780.0)
    5.  [**Distribution & Analytics:** Publish content and track organic growth ROI.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=824.0)

### Outbound Strategy: "Project Longhorn"

  - [**Context:** Developed this ABM playbook at Contently after the SDR team was laid off.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=986.0)
  - [**Target:** 186 financial institutions in Texas.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1142.0)
  - [**Goal:** Address industry challenges (e.g., brand expansion, attracting Gen Z).](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1160.0)
  - [**Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=647.0)
      - [**Multi-threading:** Targeted 6–8 contacts per account (CMO, Head of Content, VP Marketing).](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1222.0)
      - [**18–24 Touchpoint Sequence:** LinkedIn → personalized message → email sequence → phone call → voicemail → webinar invite.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1276.0)
  - [**Results:**](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1402.0)
      - [Increased qualified leads by 38%.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1409.0)
      - [Generated \~57 responses (invitation accepts, webinar attendees).](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1402.0)
      - [Landed one deal: **Pinnacle for \~$184,000**.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1353.0)

### Deal Stories & Learnings

  - [**Siemens Mexico (Dr. Churis):**](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1630.0)
      - [**Deal:** Multi-year, six-figure contract.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=507.0)
      - [**Challenge:** Multi-threaded across legal, procurement, and IT.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1630.0)
      - [**Value Prop:** Tailored to each department's pain points.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1630.0)
          - [**Legal:** Faster contract review (weeks → days).](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1713.0)
          - [**Procurement:** Tracking auto-renewals to avoid costly slips.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1733.0)
          - [**IT:** Data security and AI performance.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1694.0)
  - [**Sekisui (Plangistics):**](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1826.0)
      - [**Deal:** \~$400,000 service contract (9.5-month cycle).](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2044.0)
      - [**Challenge:** Revived a lost deal by reconnecting with a new POC.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1850.0)
      - [**Strategy:** Built a personal relationship (e.g., playing basketball) and proved ROI via a free POC.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1946.0)
      - [**Value Prop:** Reduced shipping costs ($0.80 → $0.35/lb) and provided storage for degraded resin.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=1980.0)
      - [**Learning:** Involve the economic buyer earlier to shorten the sales cycle.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2290.0)

### GrowthX Sales Model

  - [**Process:** A two-phase engagement sold upfront.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2624.0)
      - [**Phase 1: Strategy Sprint** ($30-40k flat fee, 8 weeks).](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2624.0)
      - [**Phase 2: Growth Execution** ($15k/mo retainer, 12 months).](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2624.0)
  - [**Key Metric:** Phase 2 conversion is critical, as it's the primary revenue driver.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2656.0)
  - [**Challenge:** Balancing deal closing with building foundational sales processes (e.g., demo playbooks, ICP definition).](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2488.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Tyler:** Provide feedback to Tucker.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2821.0)
  - [**Tucker:** Connect with Bridget (VP of Operations).](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2821.0)
  - [**Tucker:** Communicate next steps to Louis.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2821.0)
  - [**Louis:** Next interview is with Bridget, followed by a team presentation.](https://fathom.video/share/T8uzQwDsPTgdeXeYhYEf_x-y8WVJ8m9u?tab=summary&timestamp=2821.0)

action_items.description: Send interview feedback to Tucker re: Louis; Tucker to sync w/ Bridget and update Louis on next steps
